mcqs.q: Statement: All papers are books. Some books are pages. No page is a sheet. Conclusions: I. Some papers are pages. II. Some books are not sheets. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All papers are books' means papers are a subset of books. Step 2: 'Some books are pages' means there is an overlap between books and pages. Step 3: The overlap between books and pages may or may not include papers (I invalid). Step 4: 'Some books are pages' and 'No page is a sheet' → Those books that are pages are not sheets → Some books are not sheets (II valid).
mcqs.elimination: Only the conclusion about books not being sheets is valid.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume papers are part of the page overlap.

mcqs.q: Statement: Some trees are plants. All plants are flowers. No flower is a fruit. Conclusions: I. Some trees are not fruits. II. Some fruits are trees. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'Some trees are plants' and 'All plants are flowers' → Some trees are flowers. Step 2: 'No flower is a fruit' → The trees that are flowers are not fruits → Some trees are not fruits (I valid). Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Some fruits are trees' - Contradicts the chain. Invalid.
mcqs.elimination: Only the 'some not' conclusion is valid.
mcqs.trap: No flower is fruit, so trees that are flowers cannot be fruits.

mcqs.q: Statement: All A are B. Some B are C. No C is D. Conclusions: I. Some A are D. II. Some A are not D. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All A are B' means A is a subset of B. Step 2: 'Some B are C' means there is an overlap between B and C. Step 3: 'No C is D' means C and D are separate. Step 4: The overlap between B and C may or may not include A. Step 5: Therefore, we cannot say some A are D or some A are not D. Neither follows.
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume the B-C overlap includes A.

mcqs.q: Statement: Some doctors are engineers. All engineers are graduates. Some graduates are professors. Conclusions: I. Some doctors are professors. II. Some engineers are professors. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'Some doctors are engineers' means there is an overlap between doctors and engineers. Step 2: 'All engineers are graduates' means engineers are a subset of graduates. Step 3: 'Some graduates are professors' means there is an overlap between graduates and professors. Step 4: The overlap between graduates and professors may or may not include engineers or doctors. Step 5: Neither conclusion follows.
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Multiple 'some' and 'all' statements require careful chaining.

mcqs.q: Statement: All fruits are healthy. Some healthy are sweet. No sweet is sour. Conclusions: I. Some fruits are sweet. II. Some fruits are not sour. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All fruits are healthy' means fruits are a subset of healthy. Step 2: 'Some healthy are sweet' means there is an overlap between healthy and sweet. Step 3: The overlap between healthy and sweet may or may not include fruits (I invalid). Step 4: 'No sweet is sour' → The fruits that are sweet (if any) would not be sour, but we don't know if any fruits are sweet. However, some healthy are sweet, and fruits are healthy. Could fruits be sweet? Possibly, but not guaranteed. What about 'some fruits are not sour'? Since fruits are healthy, and some healthy are sweet, and sweet is not sour, the healthy-sweet overlap (if it includes fruits) would be not sour. But we don't know if fruits are in that overlap. So II is also not guaranteed. Let me re-evaluate: 'All fruits are healthy' and 'No sweet is sour' - fruits could be sweet or not. If they are sweet, they are not sour. If they are not sweet, they still might be sour or not. So we cannot guarantee any fruit is not sour. Neither follows.
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume fruits are in the healthy-sweet overlap.

mcqs.q: Statement: All vehicles are cars. Some cars are buses. No bus is a truck. Conclusions: I. Some vehicles are buses. II. Some vehicles are not trucks. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All vehicles are cars' means vehicles are a subset of cars. Step 2: 'Some cars are buses' means there is an overlap between cars and buses. Step 3: The overlap between cars and buses may or may not include vehicles (I invalid). Step 4: 'No bus is a truck' → The cars that are buses are not trucks. If vehicles are in that overlap, they are not trucks. But we don't know if vehicles are in the overlap. Step 5: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume vehicles are in the car-bus overlap.

mcqs.q: Statement: All oranges are fruits. Some fruits are red. All red are tasty. Conclusions: I. Some oranges are red. II. Some oranges are tasty. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All oranges are fruits' means oranges are a subset of fruits. Step 2: 'Some fruits are red' means there is an overlap between fruits and red. Step 3: The overlap between fruits and red may or may not include oranges (I invalid). Step 4: 'All red are tasty' → If oranges were red, they would be tasty. But we don't know if oranges are red. Step 5: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume oranges are in the fruit-red overlap.

mcqs.q: Statement: Some cats are dogs. All dogs are animals. No animal is a plant. Conclusions: I. Some cats are animals. II. Some cats are not plants. III. Some plants are dogs. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'Some cats are dogs' and 'All dogs are animals' → Some cats are animals (I valid). Step 2: 'No animal is a plant' → The cats that are animals are not plants → Some cats are not plants (II valid). Step 3: Conclusion III: 'Some plants are dogs' - Contradicts 'no animal is a plant' since dogs are animals. Invalid. Step 4: Only I and II follow.
mcqs.elimination: Only conclusions I and II are valid.
mcqs.trap: Dogs are animals, so no plant is a dog.

mcqs.q: Statement: All books are pages. Some pages are covers. All covers are hard. Conclusions: I. Some books are hard. II. Some pages are hard. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All books are pages' means books are a subset of pages. Step 2: 'Some pages are covers' means there is an overlap between pages and covers. Step 3: 'All covers are hard' → The pages that are covers are hard → Some pages are hard (II valid). Step 4: The overlap between pages and covers may or may not include books (I invalid). Step 5: Only Conclusion II follows.
mcqs.elimination: Only the conclusion about pages being hard is valid.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume books are in the pages-covers overlap.

mcqs.q: Statement: All A are B. No B is C. All D are C. Conclusions: I. No A is C. II. No D is A. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All A are B' and 'No B is C' → No A is C (I valid). Step 2: 'No B is C' and 'All D are C' → No D is B (since D is subset of C, and no B is C → no D is B). Step 3: 'All A are B' and 'No D is B' → No D is A (II valid). Step 4: Both conclusions follow.
mcqs.elimination: Both conclusions are valid.
mcqs.trap: Chain the negative statements correctly.

mcqs.q: Statement: Some A are B. All B are C. Some C are D. No D is E. Conclusions: I. Some A are C. II. Some A are not E. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'Some A are B' and 'All B are C' → Some A are C (I valid). Step 2: 'Some C are D' and 'No D is E' → Some C are not E. But does that mean some A are not E? The C's that are A may or may not be the C's that are D. We cannot guarantee some A are not E (II invalid). Step 3: Only Conclusion I follows.
mcqs.elimination: Only the chain between A and C is guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume the A-C overlap includes the C-D overlap.

mcqs.q: Statement: The Minister questioned the utility of the space research programme and suggested its replacement by other areas of felt national needs. Conclusions: I. Exploring the space does not contribute to critical national needs. II. Research should be oriented to national needs. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says the Minister questioned the utility of space research and suggested replacement with other national needs. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'Space does not contribute to national needs' - The Minister questioned it, but that doesn't mean it doesn't contribute. Invalid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Research should be oriented to national needs' - The Minister's suggestion implies this. Valid.[reference:48]
mcqs.elimination: The first conclusion is too strong.
mcqs.trap: Questioning utility does not prove lack of utility.

mcqs.q: Statement: The government has increased the price of fuel. Conclusions: I. The government wants to reduce fuel consumption. II. The government wants to increase revenue. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says fuel prices have been increased. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'Reduce fuel consumption' - Higher prices typically discourage consumption. Reasonable assumption. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Increase revenue' - Higher prices also generate more revenue. Reasonable assumption. Step 4: Both conclusions follow as possible assumptions.
mcqs.elimination: Both are reasonable inferences.
mcqs.trap: Multiple valid assumptions can follow from one statement.

mcqs.q: Statement: The company has decided to give bonuses to all employees this year. Conclusions: I. The company is profitable this year. II. All employees will receive bonuses. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement directly says the company has decided to give bonuses to all employees. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'The company is profitable' - Not necessarily; they may give bonuses for other reasons. Invalid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'All employees will receive bonuses' - Directly follows. Valid. Step 4: Only Conclusion II follows.
mcqs.elimination: Profitability is not guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume reasons for bonuses.

mcqs.q: Statement: Every man should have his identity card with him. That card should mention his blood group, complete address and telephone number for contact, in case, some serious accident take place. Conclusions: I. Blood cannot be transfused until its group is mentioned in the card. II. The police needs this information specially when the accident is fatal. III. In case of emergency, he may forget his address and may need the card to contact his house. IV. None is supposed to forget his phone number under any circumstances. V. When the seriously injured person is helpless to tell his blood group, this information would suffice to indicate the required blood group. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says the identity card should have blood group, address, and phone number for emergencies. Step 2: Conclusion V: 'When injured person can't tell blood group, the card would indicate it' - This is the most logical conclusion for why blood group is mentioned. Valid. Step 3: Other conclusions make assumptions not directly supported.[reference:49]
mcqs.elimination: Only the conclusion about blood group identification is clearly supported.
mcqs.trap: The card is for the person's own use in emergencies.

mcqs.q: Statement: It is not good to interfere in anyone's private life. Conclusions: I. Encroachment in anyone's personal matter is bad. II. Professional detail does not come under private life. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says it is not good to interfere in anyone's private life. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'Encroachment in personal matter is bad' - Directly restates the statement. Valid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Professional detail does not come under private life' - Not mentioned. Invalid.[reference:50]
mcqs.elimination: Professional life is not mentioned.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume what constitutes private life.

mcqs.q: Statement: Pakistan is planning to test five nuclear bombs in Baghdad. Conclusions: I. People of Pakistan are supporting the decision of the nation. II. Other nations will oppose the decision made by Pakistan. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says Pakistan is planning to test nuclear bombs. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'People of Pakistan are supporting' - Not mentioned. Invalid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Other nations will oppose' - Not mentioned. Invalid.[reference:51]
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is supported by the statement.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume public support or international reaction.

mcqs.q: Statement: Only smart and intelligent people are invited into the conference. Shakti has been invited to attend the conference. Conclusions: I. Shakti is smart. II. Shakti is intelligent. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says only smart and intelligent people are invited. Step 2: Shakti has been invited. Step 3: Therefore, Shakti is both smart and intelligent. Step 4: Both conclusions follow.[reference:52]
mcqs.elimination: Both conclusions are directly supported.
mcqs.trap: The statement explicitly requires both qualities.

mcqs.q: Statement: Company Z is making such a camera that you can take perfect photos even in bad weather. Conclusions: I. No other company has such a manufacturing camera unit. II. Any common man can take perfect shots through this camera. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says Company Z is making a camera that can take perfect photos in bad weather. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'No other company has such a unit' - Not mentioned. Invalid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Any common man can take perfect shots' - The statement talks about the camera, not the photographer. Invalid.[reference:53]
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is supported.
mcqs.trap: The camera's capability does not mean anyone can use it perfectly.

mcqs.q: Statement: The panel asked 5 members to resign from job or else they will be facing forceful rejection. Four of them have resigned till this evening. Conclusions: I. One manager will resign tomorrow. II. The panel will terminate him tomorrow. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says 5 members must resign or face termination. Step 2: Four have resigned. Step 3: The fifth will either resign (I) or be terminated (II). Step 4: Either I or II follows.[reference:54]
mcqs.elimination: Both cannot be true simultaneously.
mcqs.trap: This is an 'Either-Or' case.

mcqs.q: Statement: Fashion is a form of ugliness and becomes necessary to change every six months. Conclusions: I. Fashion designers are incapable of understanding the mind of the general public very well. II. Public go for a new change every time. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says fashion changes every six months. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'Designers are incapable' - Not mentioned. Invalid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Public go for a new change' - Since fashion changes every six months, people must go for new changes. Valid.[reference:55]
mcqs.elimination: Designer capability is not mentioned.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume reasons for fashion changes.

mcqs.q: Statement: Nepal was selected by the UNICEF executive board. Conclusions: I. Nepal has more number of children. II. Nepal has better facilities for children. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says Nepal was selected by UNICEF. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'More number of children' - Not mentioned. Invalid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Better facilities' - Not mentioned. Invalid.[reference:56]
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is supported.
mcqs.trap: Selection by UNICEF does not imply either reason.

mcqs.q: Statement: Reading makes a full man, conference a ready man and writing an exact man. Conclusions: I. The product must be good in quality. II. The price of product must be reasonable. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement is about reading, conference, and writing making a full, ready, and exact man. Step 2: The conclusions about product quality and price are completely irrelevant. Step 3: Neither conclusion follows.[reference:57]
mcqs.elimination: Both conclusions are irrelevant to the statement.
mcqs.trap: Do not force relevance where none exists.

mcqs.q: Statement: This world is neither good nor evil, each man manufactures a world for himself. Conclusions: I. Some people create a good world. II. Some people create a bad world. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says each man manufactures a world for himself but doesn't specify good or bad. Step 2: Conclusion I and II make assumptions about what kind of world people create. Step 3: Neither conclusion follows.[reference:58]
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is supported.
mcqs.trap: The statement does not specify the nature of the world each creates.

mcqs.q: Statement: Vegetables prices are soaring in the market. Conclusions: I. Vegetables are becoming a rare commodity. II. People cannot eat vegetables. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says vegetable prices are soaring. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'Vegetables are becoming rare' - High prices do not necessarily mean rarity. Invalid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'People cannot eat vegetables' - High prices do not mean people cannot eat them. Invalid.[reference:59][reference:60]
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is supported.
mcqs.trap: Price increase does not imply rarity or inability to consume.

mcqs.q: Statement: Quality has a price tag. India is allocating lots of funds to education. Conclusions: I. Quality of education in India would improve soon. II. Funding alone can enhance quality of education. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says quality has a price tag and India is allocating funds to education. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'Quality of education would improve' - Since quality requires funding and India is funding, improvement is likely. Valid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Funding alone can enhance quality' - The statement says quality has a price tag, but does not say funding alone is sufficient. Invalid.[reference:61]
mcqs.elimination: Funding alone is not stated as sufficient.
mcqs.trap: 'Price tag' means funding is necessary, not sufficient.

mcqs.q: Statement: All the organised persons find time for rest. Sunita inspite of her very busy schedule, finds time for rest. Conclusions: I. Sunita is an organised person. II. Sunita is an industrious person. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All organised persons find time for rest' → If someone finds time for rest, they may be organised, but not necessarily. However, Sunita finds time for rest despite a busy schedule. Step 2: This implies she is organised (I valid) because organised persons find time for rest. Step 3: A busy schedule implies she is industrious (II valid). Step 4: Both conclusions follow.[reference:62]
mcqs.elimination: Both conclusions are reasonable.
mcqs.trap: A busy schedule implies industriousness.

mcqs.q: Statement: In a one day cricket match, the total runs made by a team were 200. Out of these 160 runs were made by spinners. 80% of the runs were made by spinners. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says 160 out of 200 runs were made by spinners. Step 2: This means 80% of runs were from spinners. Step 3: This does not imply the team has good spinners (they could have scored runs by chance). Step 4: It does not imply poor batsmen (batsmen might have made the remaining 40 runs). Step 5: It does not imply the team has only spinners. Step 6: None of these conclusions follow.[reference:63]
mcqs.elimination: None of the conclusions are logically supported.
mcqs.trap: Statistics do not imply quality or composition.

mcqs.q: Statement: The manager humiliated Sachin in the presence of his colleagues. Conclusions: I. The manager did not like Sachin. II. Sachin was not popular with his colleagues. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: The statement says the manager humiliated Sachin. Step 2: This does not prove the manager disliked Sachin (could be other reasons). Step 3: It does not say anything about Sachin's popularity with colleagues. Step 4: Neither conclusion follows.
mcqs.elimination: Both conclusions add new information.
mcqs.trap: Humiliation does not prove dislike or unpopularity.

mcqs.q: Statement: A man must be wise to be a good wrangler. Good wranglers are talkative and boring. Conclusions: I. All the wise persons are boring. II. All the talkative persons are wise. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'A man must be wise to be a good wrangler' → All good wranglers are wise. Step 2: 'Good wranglers are talkative and boring' → All good wranglers are talkative and boring. Step 3: Conclusion I: 'All wise persons are boring' - Reverses the logic. Invalid. Step 4: Conclusion II: 'All talkative persons are wise' - Reverses the logic. Invalid.[reference:64]
mcqs.elimination: Both conclusions reverse the statements.
mcqs.trap: Do not reverse 'All A are B' to 'All B are A'.

mcqs.q: Statement: All good athletes win. All good athletes eat well. Conclusions: I. All those who eat well are good athletes. II. All those who win eat well. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Both statements say good athletes have two properties: win and eat well. Step 2: This does not mean everyone who wins or eats well is a good athlete. Step 3: Neither conclusion follows.[reference:65]
mcqs.elimination: Both conclusions reverse the statements.
mcqs.trap: All A are B does not mean all B are A.

mcqs.q: Statement: All pens are chalks. All chairs are chalks. Conclusions: I. Some pens are chairs. II. Some chalks are pens. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'All pens are chalks' and 'All chairs are chalks'. Step 2: Both pens and chairs are subsets of chalks. Step 3: Conclusion I: 'Some pens are chairs' - Not guaranteed. Invalid. Step 4: Conclusion II: 'Some chalks are pens' - Since all pens are chalks, some chalks are pens. Valid.[reference:66]
mcqs.elimination: Two subsets of the same set may not overlap.
mcqs.trap: Do not assume overlap between pens and chairs.

mcqs.q: Statement: All fans are small. All small are rats. Conclusions: I. All fans are rats. II. Some rats are fans. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Chain: fans → small → rats. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'All fans are rats' - Valid by chain rule. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Some rats are fans' - Since all fans are rats, some rats are fans. Valid.[reference:67]
mcqs.elimination: Both conclusions are supported.
mcqs.trap: All A are B implies some B are A.

mcqs.q: Statement: No bird is a crow. Some crows are black. Conclusions: I. At least some birds are black. II. Some black which are crows are definitely not birds. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'No bird is a crow' means birds and crows are separate. Step 2: 'Some crows are black' means there are black crows. Step 3: Conclusion I: 'Some birds are black' - No link between birds and black. Invalid. Step 4: Conclusion II: 'Some black which are crows are not birds' - Since no bird is a crow, black crows are not birds. Valid.[reference:68]
mcqs.elimination: There is no link between birds and black.
mcqs.trap: Crows being black does not mean birds are black.

mcqs.q: Statement: Some leaves are roots. Some roots are stems. All flowers are stems. Conclusions: I. No flower is a leaf. II. Some leaves are stem. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: 'Some leaves are roots' and 'Some roots are stems' → No guaranteed link between leaves and stems. Step 2: 'All flowers are stems' → Flowers are a subset of stems. Step 3: Conclusion I: 'No flower is a leaf' - Not guaranteed. Invalid. Step 4: Conclusion II: 'Some leaves are stem' - Not guaranteed (the roots that are stems may not be the roots that are leaves). Invalid.[reference:69]
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Two 'some' statements do not guarantee a connection.

mcqs.q: Statement: Some forests are huts. Some huts are walls. Some walls are nets. Conclusions: I. Some nets are forests. II. Some nets are huts. Which conclusion follows?
mcqs.exp: Step 1: Three 'some' statements with no guaranteed overlap between non-adjacent terms. Step 2: Conclusion I: 'Some nets are forests' - No link between nets and forests. Invalid. Step 3: Conclusion II: 'Some nets are huts' - No link between nets and huts. Invalid.[reference:70]
mcqs.elimination: Neither conclusion is guaranteed.
mcqs.trap: Multiple 'some' statements do not create chains.
